questions.prompt: What does version control like Git give you?
options: It runs your tests automatically every time you save
It records snapshots of your files over time so you can see history and roll back
It replaces your editor with a built-in one
It deletes old versions of files to save disk space
questions.explanation: Git records a snapshot each time you commit, letting you inspect history, compare versions, and undo changes. It does not run tests or remove old versions.

options: A folder that Git is watching
A saved snapshot of your project with a message describing what changed
A copy of your files on a remote server
A list of files Git should ignore
questions.explanation: A commit is a point-in-time snapshot with a message. History is the chain of commits you can inspect or revert to.

questions.prompt: What is the role of the staging area?
options: It is where Git stores deleted files
It is a holding zone where you choose the exact changes a commit will include
It is the remote copy of your repository
It automatically commits every change you make
questions.explanation: You git add the specific changes you want, and git commit turns just those staged changes into a snapshot. This lets one commit hold only the changes you intend.

questions.prompt: You edit a file but have not committed. What is true?
options: Only the working tree changed; history is unaffected until you commit
The change is lost as soon as you close the editor
Git automatically commits it after a few seconds
questions.explanation: Editing changes only the working tree (the files on disk). Nothing enters history until you stage and commit, which is why git status shows uncommitted changes.
